meta.title: DBX-CEA-1: System Prompts, RAG, Memory and MCP Tools

questions.stem: An agent must always respond in JSON with fields `answer` and `citations`. Where should that requirement be placed for the MOST consistent behavior?

options.text: In the system prompt or equivalent top-level instructions
options.explanation: Persistent formatting and behavioral constraints belong in the system-level instructions, which govern every turn of the conversation regardless of retrieved content or user phrasing.

options.text: Only in the final user message of each session
options.explanation: Relying on the final user message means the constraint can be missing or inconsistently worded across sessions.

options.text: Inside retrieved document chunks
options.explanation: Retrieved chunks are variable and cannot reliably enforce output format on every response.

options.text: In a SQL warehouse column comment
options.explanation: Warehouse column comments are metadata annotations, not agent instruction surfaces.

questions.stem: A prompt engineer notices that the system prompt contains several paragraphs of persona description that do not change agent outputs on any benchmark task. What is the BEST action?

options.text: Add even more persona detail to increase model engagement
options.explanation: Adding more decorative text wastes context tokens without improving task quality.

options.text: Remove the decorative persona text and keep only instructions that materially change behavior
options.explanation: Context engineering focuses on maximizing useful signal in the context window; decorative persona text that does not affect outputs consumes budget without benefit.

options.text: Move the persona paragraphs into every retrieved chunk
options.explanation: Injecting persona text into retrieved chunks wastes retrieval context and conflates instructions with source material.

options.text: Convert the persona text to chain-of-thought scratchpad instructions
options.explanation: Chain-of-thought instructions are useful only when reasoning traces demonstrably improve output; converting irrelevant persona text to CoT still wastes tokens.

questions.stem: An agent is given the instruction: "If the user asks about refunds, always escalate to a human agent." This instruction is not working reliably. Which change is MOST likely to improve compliance?

options.text: Place the escalation rule in a clearly delimited section at the top of the system prompt with explicit trigger conditions
options.explanation: Explicit, clearly delimited rules with concrete trigger conditions at the top of the system prompt are processed most reliably. Burying rules in prose reduces compliance.

options.text: Remove the instruction and rely on the model's pretraining to handle refund requests
options.explanation: Pretrained models do not have organization-specific escalation policies and will not consistently apply them.

options.text: Include the escalation rule only in the RAG retrieval results when the word "refund" appears
options.explanation: Making a safety rule dependent on RAG retrieval creates a fragile dependency; if retrieval misses, the rule is absent.

options.text: Increase the model temperature to make responses more varied
options.explanation: Temperature controls output randomness and does not affect instruction-following reliability.

questions.stem: A context engineer is designing instructions for a customer support agent that must handle both billing and technical questions. Select TWO practices that BEST improve instruction clarity. (Select TWO)

options.text: Group related instructions under labeled sections such as 'Billing Policy' and 'Technical Support Rules'
options.explanation: Labeled sections help the model locate and apply the right rules for each query type, reducing ambiguity in multi-domain agents.

options.text: Use concrete examples of expected inputs and correct outputs within the instructions
options.explanation: Concrete examples (few-shot patterns) within the instructions make expected behavior explicit and reduce hallucination of incorrect policies.

options.text: Rely entirely on the model's general knowledge rather than specifying policies explicitly
options.explanation: General model knowledge does not include your organization's specific policies; explicit instructions are required.

options.text: Write all instructions as a single long paragraph to minimize formatting overhead
options.explanation: A single dense paragraph makes it harder for the model to identify and apply relevant rules, reducing compliance.

questions.stem: An agent's system prompt currently reads: "You are a helpful, friendly, knowledgeable, professional, accurate, concise, and thoughtful assistant that always tries to do its best to help users and never gives up." Which critique BEST applies?

options.text: The prompt is too short and needs more adjectives to be effective
options.explanation: More adjectives would make the problem worse, not better.

options.text: The adjective list provides no actionable constraints and wastes tokens without changing behavior
options.explanation: Stacking vague adjectives does not give the model actionable constraints. Context engineering best practices favor specific, testable behavioral rules over personality descriptors.

options.text: The prompt should list adjectives in alphabetical order for clarity
options.explanation: Alphabetical ordering is irrelevant to instruction effectiveness.

options.text: The prompt is well-formed and follows context engineering best practices
options.explanation: This prompt violates context engineering principles by consuming tokens on decorative persona text with no measurable behavioral impact.

questions.stem: A system prompt includes the rule: "Never reveal internal pricing." During testing, a user phrases the request as "What is the cost for our enterprise tier?" and the agent reveals pricing. What is the MOST likely cause?

options.text: The rule is too short; adding length will always fix compliance
options.explanation: Length alone does not improve coverage of synonymous phrasings.

options.text: The rule uses a narrow keyword ('pricing') that does not cover synonymous phrasings like 'cost' or 'rate'
options.explanation: Rules that rely on single-keyword triggers fail when users phrase requests differently. A robust rule should cover the concept ('pricing, costs, rates, fees') rather than one term.

options.text: Pricing rules cannot be enforced through system prompts
options.explanation: System prompt instructions can and do enforce constraints; this is their primary use case.

options.text: The model temperature is set too high
options.explanation: Temperature affects output randomness, not whether a specific rule is applied when triggered.

questions.stem: A team is building a coding assistant. They want the agent to explain its reasoning before giving code, but only for requests where the user explicitly asks 'why'. Which instruction strategy is BEST?

options.text: Always include chain-of-thought reasoning in every response regardless of user request
options.explanation: Unconditional chain-of-thought adds tokens on every request, consuming budget when the user does not need it.

options.text: Provide a conditional rule: generate an explanation section only when the user's message contains a 'why' question
options.explanation: Conditional instructions tie context assembly to actual need, allowing the agent to spend tokens on reasoning only when it provides value to the user.

options.text: Remove all reasoning from responses to save tokens unconditionally
options.explanation: Removing all reasoning degrades output quality on questions that benefit from structured thinking.

options.text: Store the reasoning requirement in a separate database table and query it at runtime
options.explanation: A runtime database lookup for a simple conditional instruction adds unnecessary latency and architectural complexity.

questions.stem: A team stores all company policies as a vector index. At query time, the top-3 relevant policy chunks are inserted into the system prompt. What is the PRIMARY risk of this design?

options.text: Retrieval failures or low similarity scores may cause critical policies to be omitted from the prompt
options.explanation: Dynamically retrieved policies depend on retrieval quality. If the query is phrased unusually or the embedding similarity is low, the relevant policy may not appear in the top-3, leaving the agent without critical constraints.

options.text: The vector index will automatically update the system prompt at training time
options.explanation: Vector indexes influence inference-time prompts, not model training weights.

options.text: Using top-3 retrieval always returns too many tokens for any model
options.explanation: Whether top-3 is too many tokens depends on chunk size and is not universally true; this is not the primary risk.

options.text: Vector search cannot be integrated with system prompts
options.explanation: Vector search can absolutely be integrated with dynamic system prompt construction.

questions.stem: A team wants a managed semantic search index that automatically stays synchronized with a Delta table as rows are added or updated. Which Databricks feature BEST satisfies this requirement?

options.text: A notebook-scoped pandas DataFrame loaded at startup
options.explanation: A pandas DataFrame is in-memory and does not support automatic synchronization with a Delta table or semantic search.

options.text: A Databricks Vector Search index with delta-sync enabled
options.explanation: Databricks Vector Search with delta-sync keeps a vector index automatically synchronized with a source Delta table, supporting both incremental inserts and updates without manual re-indexing.

options.text: A SQL warehouse result cache
options.explanation: A SQL warehouse result cache stores query results for repeat SQL execution, not semantic vector embeddings.

options.text: An MLflow model registry alias
options.explanation: An MLflow model registry alias points to a specific model version; it is not a retrieval index.

questions.stem: Search quality drops because each chunk contains many unrelated topics from long documents. Which change is MOST likely to improve retrieval precision?

options.text: Store every document as one very large chunk to maximize coverage
options.explanation: Large monolithic chunks embed multiple unrelated topics into a single vector, causing low-quality similarity matches.

options.text: Disable embeddings and switch to row-level security filters only
options.explanation: Row-level security is an access-control mechanism, not a relevance signal. Disabling embeddings would eliminate semantic search entirely.

options.text: Use a chunking strategy that preserves local semantic coherence, such as splitting at topic boundaries
options.explanation: Chunking at semantic boundaries ensures each chunk's embedding represents one coherent topic, which dramatically improves retrieval precision and recall.

options.text: Increase the SQL warehouse cluster size
options.explanation: Compute cluster size affects query performance, not embedding quality or chunk relevance.

questions.stem: A retrieval system needs to surface both semantically similar documents and documents with exact product code matches such as `SKU-40291`. Select TWO approaches that BEST address this requirement. (Select TWO)

options.text: Use hybrid retrieval that combines dense vector similarity with sparse keyword (BM25) signals
options.explanation: Hybrid retrieval merges dense vector search (good for semantic similarity) with sparse BM25 (good for exact token matches like SKU codes), improving coverage for both query types.

options.text: Apply a re-ranker model to re-score the merged candidate set before returning final results
options.explanation: A re-ranker scores the merged candidate set using a more powerful cross-encoder, improving the final ordering before results are inserted into context.

options.text: Remove metadata fields from the index to simplify the schema
options.explanation: Removing metadata fields reduces the index's filtering and scoring capabilities, making exact-match retrieval harder.

options.text: Raise the generation temperature to increase response variety
options.explanation: Generation temperature controls output randomness during text generation and has no effect on retrieval quality.

questions.stem: A RAG agent is returning answers that cite irrelevant paragraphs. The retrieval logs show high similarity scores for the wrong chunks. What is the MOST likely root cause?

options.text: The embedding model is misaligned with the domain vocabulary; a domain-adapted embedding model would better capture relevance
options.explanation: High similarity scores for wrong chunks indicate the embedding space is not aligned with domain-specific meaning. Specialized or fine-tuned embedding models better represent domain vocabulary and improve retrieval precision.

options.text: The SQL warehouse is auto-stopping too quickly between requests
options.explanation: Warehouse auto-stop affects compute availability, not vector similarity scoring.

options.text: The agent's generation temperature is too low
options.explanation: Temperature is a generation parameter and does not influence which documents are retrieved.

options.text: The Unity Catalog permissions are too restrictive for the embedding service
options.explanation: Unity Catalog permissions control access, not similarity ranking.

questions.stem: A team builds a RAG pipeline that retrieves the top-20 chunks and passes all of them to the model. Users report slow responses and sometimes incoherent answers. Which improvement is MOST targeted?

options.text: Reduce top-k to a smaller set of the most relevant chunks and apply a re-ranker to ensure quality
options.explanation: Passing 20 chunks floods the context window with low-relevance material, increasing latency and diluting signal. Reducing top-k with re-ranking provides fewer, higher-quality chunks.

options.text: Increase the vector index size to allow more candidate documents
options.explanation: A larger index would increase the pool of candidates, not reduce the number passed to the model.

options.text: Switch to a SQL-only retrieval strategy with no embeddings
options.explanation: SQL retrieval retrieves structured rows, not unstructured document chunks, and does not solve the chunk-volume problem.

options.text: Move all retrieved chunks into the system prompt section unchanged
options.explanation: Moving chunks to the system prompt section does not reduce their count or improve relevance.

questions.stem: A retrieval pipeline embeds document chunks using a general-purpose embedding model but user queries are highly technical and use domain acronyms not common in general web text. What change would MOST improve retrieval quality?

options.text: Re-embed the corpus using a domain-adapted embedding model trained on similar technical content
options.explanation: General-purpose embedding models underrepresent domain-specific acronyms and terminology. A domain-adapted model produces embeddings that better align query and document representations for specialized vocabulary.

options.text: Increase the chunk overlap window from 50 to 100 tokens
options.explanation: Chunk overlap affects boundary continuity, not the quality of embedding representation for domain terms.

options.text: Use a larger SQL cluster for embedding computation
options.explanation: Cluster size affects compute speed, not embedding quality.

options.text: Disable cosine similarity and switch to Euclidean distance
options.explanation: The choice of distance metric is a secondary tuning lever; the root cause is embedding misalignment, not the similarity metric.

questions.stem: A Vector Search query filters by `department = 'finance'` before performing semantic search. What is the PRIMARY benefit of this approach?

options.text: Pre-filtering restricts the candidate pool to relevant documents, reducing noise in retrieved results and improving precision
options.explanation: Metadata pre-filtering scopes the similarity search to a relevant subset, preventing cross-department documents from occupying top-k slots and improving both precision and context quality.

options.text: Pre-filtering replaces the need for embeddings entirely
options.explanation: Pre-filtering narrows the search space but does not replace the semantic similarity computation over the filtered set.

options.text: Pre-filtering increases the total number of results returned
options.explanation: Pre-filtering constrains the candidate pool; it typically reduces, not increases, the number of potential results.

options.text: Pre-filtering eliminates latency because no vector comparison is performed
options.explanation: Vector comparison is still performed over the filtered subset; latency is reduced but not eliminated.

questions.stem: A support agent must remember user-stated preferences such as preferred language and communication style across sessions that occur days apart. Which memory design is MOST appropriate?

options.text: Keep all historical conversation turns in the active prompt for every new session
options.explanation: Keeping all historical turns in the prompt grows unboundedly and becomes impractical after many sessions.

options.text: Persist a structured user profile in an external store such as Lakebase and retrieve it at session start
options.explanation: Cross-session preferences require durable external persistence. A structured user profile stored in Lakebase or another persistent store can be fetched at session start and injected into the context selectively.

options.text: Rely only on the model's pretrained knowledge to infer user preferences
options.explanation: Pretrained models have no knowledge of individual user preferences and cannot retrieve them from training weights.

options.text: Use a larger SQL warehouse cluster to hold more in-memory context
options.explanation: A larger SQL warehouse affects compute throughput, not conversational memory persistence.

questions.stem: An agent's conversation history is growing so large that it causes context overflow. The agent still needs to act on decisions made in earlier turns. Which technique BEST addresses this?

options.text: Delete all history after every message to keep the context short
options.explanation: Deleting all history loses prior decisions the agent needs for coherent multi-turn behavior.

options.text: Summarize older turns into a compact state representation and retain only recent verbatim exchanges
options.explanation: Summarization compresses older turns into a dense state description, preserving decision-relevant information while dramatically reducing token usage.

options.text: Duplicate the full transcript in both the system prompt and the user message
options.explanation: Duplicating the transcript worsens the overflow problem by consuming more tokens.

options.text: Move the entire conversation history into Unity Catalog column comments
options.explanation: Unity Catalog column comments are metadata for data assets, not a conversational memory mechanism.

questions.stem: A team wants to track agent reasoning state across a long multi-step workflow where the agent calls several tools in sequence. Which approach BEST supports durable state between tool calls?

options.text: Store intermediate results and reasoning summaries in an external key-value or document store and pass only the current step's context to the model
options.explanation: Storing intermediate state externally and injecting only the relevant summary into each model call keeps the context lean while preserving workflow continuity across many steps.

options.text: Include the full output of every previous tool call in the next model invocation
options.explanation: Including full tool outputs from every prior step grows the context linearly with workflow length, eventually causing overflow.

options.text: Require users to repeat prior tool outputs in each new message
options.explanation: Requiring users to repeat prior outputs creates friction and is error-prone.

options.text: Use a larger embedding model to encode all prior tool outputs into a single vector
options.explanation: A single embedding vector cannot faithfully encode all the structured details from multiple tool outputs, and it does not help with context window management.

questions.stem: A financial advisory agent must distinguish between facts the user stated in the current session versus facts learned in previous sessions. Which design supports this distinction MOST reliably?

options.text: Store all session facts inline in the chat history without any labeling or tagging
options.explanation: Unlabeled inline facts give the model no signal about recency or source, making it harder to apply appropriate confidence or staleness checks.

options.text: Maintain separate memory scopes: a labeled in-session scratchpad for current-turn facts and a labeled long-term profile for cross-session facts
options.explanation: Distinct labeled memory scopes let the model and the engineer apply different confidence levels, update frequencies, and staleness logic to in-session versus long-term facts.

options.text: Use a single flat memory block and rely on the model to infer fact provenance
options.explanation: A flat block without scope labels requires the model to infer provenance, which it cannot do reliably.

options.text: Retrieve the full user history on every query regardless of relevance to avoid missing any detail
options.explanation: Retrieving all history on every query wastes context tokens and may surface stale or irrelevant information.

questions.stem: A team is designing an agent that must log conversation state for compliance and also support fast in-session recall. Select TWO storage choices that BEST serve these dual needs. (Select TWO)

options.text: Write structured conversation state to a Delta table for durable, auditable storage
options.explanation: Delta tables provide ACID transactions, time-travel, and audit history, satisfying compliance requirements for durable conversation logs.

options.text: Use a low-latency key-value cache such as Redis for fast in-session state reads
options.explanation: A low-latency cache provides sub-millisecond reads of recent session state, keeping agent response times fast without re-querying the Delta table on every turn.

options.text: Embed the full conversation history in the model weights through fine-tuning after each session
options.explanation: Fine-tuning the model after every session is prohibitively slow, expensive, and does not support real-time state updates.

options.text: Store everything in a temporary notebook variable that is cleared when the cluster restarts
options.explanation: Notebook variables are ephemeral and cleared at cluster restart, providing no durability for compliance logging.

questions.stem: MLflow is used to track an agent's experiment runs across a multi-step evaluation workflow. What is the PRIMARY benefit of using MLflow for this purpose?

options.text: MLflow provides reproducible run tracking with logged parameters, metrics, and artifacts that allow state to be inspected and replayed
options.explanation: MLflow's run tracking records parameters, metrics, and artifacts with timestamps, enabling inspection, debugging, and reproducibility of agent behavior across steps.

options.text: MLflow automatically summarizes conversation history into compact prompts
options.explanation: MLflow does not automatically summarize conversation history; it stores what is explicitly logged by the application.

options.text: MLflow stores embeddings in a vector index for semantic retrieval of past runs
options.explanation: MLflow is a model lifecycle platform; vector storage and retrieval are handled by Databricks Vector Search.

options.text: MLflow generates the system prompt automatically from past run metadata
options.explanation: MLflow does not generate system prompts; prompt management is the engineer's responsibility.

questions.stem: What is the PRIMARY value of defining a well-structured tool schema (name, description, parameters with types) for an agent?

options.text: It guarantees the model will never produce incorrect output when the tool is used
options.explanation: A schema improves reliability but does not guarantee correct outputs; the model can still reason incorrectly about when or how to use the tool.

options.text: It allows the model to understand the tool's purpose and call signature so it can invoke the tool correctly
options.explanation: A clear schema with names, descriptions, and typed parameters gives the model the information it needs to decide when and how to call the tool correctly.

options.text: It replaces authentication and access-control mechanisms for the tool
options.explanation: Authentication and governance are infrastructure concerns separate from the schema definition.

options.text: It eliminates the need for system prompt instructions that mention the tool
options.explanation: System prompt instructions about when and why to use tools complement the schema and should not be eliminated.

questions.stem: A governed analytics agent must query sensitive sales tables. The security team requires that all queries be subject to row-level security and column masking. Which design BEST meets this requirement?

options.text: Give the model unrestricted SQL access to all catalogs so it can find the data it needs
options.explanation: Unrestricted catalog access bypasses row-level security and masking, violating the security requirement.

options.text: Embed database credentials directly in the system prompt for the model's convenience
options.explanation: Embedding credentials in the system prompt creates a significant security risk; credentials should be managed by secrets management systems.

options.text: Expose curated Unity Catalog functions or governed SQL tools that enforce row-level security and masking policies
options.explanation: Unity Catalog-backed functions and governed SQL tools automatically enforce row-level security, column masking, and audit logging on every query the agent makes.

options.text: Have the model read cloud storage files directly to bypass warehouse latency
options.explanation: Reading cloud storage files directly bypasses Unity Catalog governance entirely.

questions.stem: A team is integrating external REST APIs as agent tools. Select TWO practices that BEST improve reliability and debuggability. (Select TWO)

options.text: Validate tool call inputs against the declared parameter schema before sending the API request
options.explanation: Pre-call schema validation prevents malformed requests from reaching the API, reducing errors and wasted latency.

options.text: Return structured error responses with actionable messages so the agent can retry or ask for clarification
options.explanation: Structured error responses give the model enough context to recover, retry with corrected parameters, or surface a meaningful message to the user.

options.text: Suppress all tool call logs to minimize storage overhead
options.explanation: Suppressing logs removes the observability needed to diagnose failures in production.

options.text: Accept all inputs as a single untyped free-text string to simplify the schema
options.explanation: A single free-text string provides no type safety or schema guidance, making it harder for the model to construct correct calls.

questions.stem: An agent using the Model Context Protocol (MCP) needs to expose a company's internal knowledge base as a resource server. What does MCP's resource abstraction primarily allow?

options.text: MCP resources allow a server to expose structured or unstructured content that a host agent can read into context without embedding retrieval logic in the agent itself
options.explanation: MCP's resource abstraction lets a server expose any content (files, database rows, API responses) as named resources that a host can fetch and inject into context, decoupling retrieval implementation from agent logic.

options.text: MCP resources replace the need for a vector index by storing all documents inside the protocol layer
options.explanation: MCP is a protocol for exposing capabilities; the underlying storage and retrieval (e.g., a vector index) still exists on the server side.

options.text: MCP resources are exclusively used for streaming audio and video media content
options.explanation: MCP resources are a general-purpose abstraction for any content type, not specifically for media streams.

options.text: MCP resources automatically summarize and compress documents before returning them to the agent
options.explanation: MCP resources deliver content as-is; summarization would need to be implemented explicitly on the server.

questions.stem: An MCP server exposes a tool called `run_sql` that executes arbitrary SQL on a data warehouse. A security reviewer flags this as high risk. What is the BEST mitigation?

options.text: Restrict the tool to a predefined set of parameterized query templates and enforce Unity Catalog permissions on the service account
options.explanation: Parameterized query templates prevent SQL injection by separating code from data, while Unity Catalog permissions limit the blast radius of any misuse to only the tables the service account is authorized to access.

options.text: Document the risk in a README and leave the tool implementation unchanged
options.explanation: Documenting a risk without mitigating it leaves the vulnerability active in production.

options.text: Increase the model temperature to randomize SQL generation and reduce attack predictability
options.explanation: Temperature controls output randomness during text generation and has no effect on whether a SQL string is safe to execute.

options.text: Move the tool from MCP to a direct function call to improve execution performance
options.explanation: Changing the invocation mechanism (MCP vs. direct call) does not address the security risk of arbitrary SQL execution.

questions.stem: An agent has access to 12 tools. Users report that it frequently chooses the wrong tool for a task. What is the MOST effective prompt-level fix?

options.text: Improve each tool's description to clearly state its purpose, expected inputs, and the situations where it should be preferred over similar tools
options.explanation: The model selects tools based on their name and description in the schema. Clearer, more distinctive descriptions that highlight when each tool should be preferred reduce selection errors without changing the tool set.

options.text: Pre-populate all 12 tool outputs in the system prompt regardless of whether they are relevant
options.explanation: Pre-populating all tool outputs wastes tokens and creates noise; tools should be called based on need, not pre-loaded.

options.text: Reduce the number of tools to 1 to eliminate routing errors entirely
options.explanation: Reducing functionality to fix routing errors is not a viable solution for a multi-capability agent.

options.text: Use a higher model temperature to improve tool selection diversity
options.explanation: Higher temperature increases randomness in tool selection, which would worsen routing accuracy.

questions.stem: An agent uses a tool that writes records to a production database. During a demonstration, the agent incorrectly calls this tool with test data. Which safeguard would have BEST prevented this?

options.text: Require explicit human confirmation before any tool call that performs a write or delete operation
options.explanation: A human-in-the-loop confirmation gate for write and delete operations ensures that potentially irreversible side effects are approved by a person before execution, which is the standard safeguard for destructive tool calls.

options.text: Grant the agent read-only database access so all write calls automatically fail with an error
options.explanation: Read-only access would prevent the agent from performing legitimate writes as well, removing the feature entirely rather than adding a safeguard.

options.text: Increase the top-k retrieval to reduce the chance of the agent confusing test and production contexts
options.explanation: Retrieval breadth affects answer quality, not whether a write tool is called incorrectly.

options.text: Use a domain-adapted embedding model for the write tool's description
options.explanation: Embedding models are for retrieval; they have no role in write-tool invocation logic.

questions.stem: Which statement BEST describes what must be managed within a model's context window in a production agent?

options.text: Only the most recent user message counts toward the context window limit
options.explanation: All components of the prompt contribute to context token usage, not just the latest user message.

options.text: The system prompt, conversation history, retrieved chunks, tool call inputs and outputs, and the current user message all consume context tokens
options.explanation: Every element passed to the model — system prompt, history, retrieved context, tool traces, and the new user message — competes for the same finite context window budget.

options.text: Context limits apply only to output tokens, not input tokens
options.explanation: Both input and output tokens count toward context limits; input tokens fill the context window and output tokens are counted separately against the max output limit.

options.text: SQL warehouse result caches eliminate context window constraints for production agents
options.explanation: Result caches store query outputs and have no effect on model context limits.

questions.stem: An agent's context window overflows after 8 turns because tool call traces from prior steps are never pruned. Which strategy MOST directly solves this?

options.text: Prune or summarize completed tool traces after the agent has processed their results, retaining only the key outcome
options.explanation: Completed tool traces whose results have already been incorporated can be compressed to just their outcome (e.g., 'searched for X, found Y'), dramatically reducing token accumulation across long workflows.

options.text: Increase the model's generation temperature to shorten output length
options.explanation: Temperature is a generation parameter and does not affect how many tokens tool traces consume in the input.

options.text: Store tool traces in a Unity Catalog table so they are not in the prompt
options.explanation: Storing traces externally still requires fetching and injecting them into context if needed; the token cost is not reduced by their storage location alone.

options.text: Disable tool calling after turn 4 to prevent further trace accumulation
options.explanation: Disabling tool calling removes agent capability as a workaround rather than addressing the token accumulation problem.

questions.stem: A team wants to allocate context tokens across system instructions, retrieved chunks, and conversation history. Which approach BEST prevents any one component from crowding out the others?

options.text: Define explicit maximum token budgets for each context section and enforce them programmatically before model invocation
options.explanation: Explicit token budgets per section give the engineer deterministic control over what enters the context window, preventing retrieval or history from crowding out critical instructions.

options.text: Use the largest possible model to avoid worrying about token limits
options.explanation: Using a larger model raises costs and still requires good context management; even large models have finite context windows.

options.text: Let the model decide dynamically how many tokens to allocate to each section
options.explanation: The model does not control how many tokens are in its input; that is determined by the prompt assembly code before the API call.

options.text: Combine all sections into one undelimited block so the model can self-organize the content
options.explanation: An undelimited block makes it harder for the model to distinguish section types and easier for one section to overflow into another.

questions.stem: A long-running research agent accumulates 80,000 tokens of context after 20 steps. The model's limit is 32,000 tokens. Which compaction approach preserves the most decision-relevant information?

options.text: Truncate the oldest tokens blindly until the total fits within the limit
options.explanation: Blind truncation removes the oldest tokens, which often include the original task specification and key early decisions that are still relevant.

options.text: Use a structured compaction step that summarizes completed reasoning phases, retains open goals and key findings, and discards redundant intermediate steps
options.explanation: Structured compaction intelligently compresses completed phases into summaries while preserving open goals, key findings, and decisions that remain actionable, resulting in a much more useful compressed context than blind truncation.

options.text: Delete the entire conversation and ask the user to restart from scratch
options.explanation: Restarting discards all accumulated work and creates a poor user experience.

options.text: Upgrade to a model with a 128k context limit and make no other changes
options.explanation: While a larger context model helps, relying solely on model upgrades does not solve the architectural problem for workflows that grow beyond any fixed limit.

questions.stem: A team is assembling context for a customer service agent. Select TWO techniques that BEST help stay within the context budget while preserving answer quality. (Select TWO)

options.text: Retrieve fewer but higher-quality chunks using a re-ranker rather than always passing the top-20 results
options.explanation: A re-ranker selects the highest-quality subset of retrieved candidates, reducing token usage while improving signal-to-noise in retrieved context.

options.text: Summarize conversation history older than the last 3 turns into a compact state block
options.explanation: Summarizing older history compresses verbose conversation logs into a compact representation, freeing tokens for fresh retrieved context and the current turn.

options.text: Include all available policy documents in every prompt regardless of their relevance to the current query
options.explanation: Including all policy documents regardless of relevance floods the context with noise, reducing both quality and staying within the token budget.

options.text: Increase the system prompt length to cover every possible edge case in a single static document
options.explanation: A longer system prompt consumes more fixed tokens on every request, worsening the budget problem across all queries.

questions.stem: A team observes that their agent's response quality degrades when the retrieved context section exceeds 6,000 tokens even though the model supports 100k tokens total. What phenomenon BEST explains this?

options.text: Models have difficulty attending to information buried in the middle of a very long context, sometimes called the 'lost in the middle' effect
options.explanation: Research shows that transformer models tend to attend more reliably to information at the beginning and end of a long context, with material in the middle receiving less attention — this degrades answer quality when critical context is buried in the middle of a large retrieval block.

options.text: Retrieved context above 6,000 tokens automatically triggers an expensive model fine-tuning job
options.explanation: Retrieval context length does not trigger fine-tuning; fine-tuning is a separate offline training process.

options.text: The SQL warehouse throttles API requests that contain more than 6,000 tokens of payload
options.explanation: SQL warehouse throttling is related to compute resources, not the content length of API payloads.

options.text: Longer retrieved contexts force the model to use a smaller output vocabulary
options.explanation: Vocabulary size is fixed by the tokenizer and does not change based on context length.

questions.stem: Which of the following is the MOST direct consequence of exhausting a model's context window during a multi-turn conversation?

options.text: The model automatically switches to a larger model tier to accommodate additional tokens
options.explanation: Context overflow does not trigger automatic model upgrades; that is a separate infrastructure decision made by the operator.

options.text: Older content must be dropped or the request will fail, potentially losing important context
options.explanation: The context window is a hard limit. When exceeded, the application must either truncate or summarize older content, or the API call will fail with a token limit error — either outcome risks losing important conversational context.

options.text: The model dynamically compresses its weights at inference time to fit more tokens
options.explanation: Model weights are fixed after training and cannot be dynamically compressed at inference time.

options.text: The SQL warehouse increases its memory allocation to compensate for the overflow
options.explanation: SQL warehouse memory and model context windows are independent systems with no connection.

questions.stem: A team wants to compare two RAG configurations — one with top-5 retrieval and one with top-10 retrieval — using a fixed set of 200 evaluation questions. Which evaluation approach is MOST rigorous?

options.text: Run both configurations against all 200 questions, compute answer quality metrics for each, and perform a statistical comparison
options.explanation: Running both configurations against the same benchmark and comparing metrics provides statistically meaningful, reproducible evidence for configuration decisions.

options.text: Ask a single engineer to review 5 example outputs from each configuration and pick a winner
options.explanation: A single engineer reviewing 5 examples is not statistically valid and introduces personal bias.

options.text: Deploy the preferred configuration to production immediately and monitor user satisfaction scores
options.explanation: Deploying untested changes to production exposes all users to potential quality regressions before the configuration is validated.

options.text: Evaluate only response latency because answer quality is too subjective to measure objectively
options.explanation: Answer quality can be measured using metrics such as faithfulness, groundedness, and answer relevance; dismissing quality measurement is a false constraint.

questions.stem: A governance review is focused on preventing sensitive PII from entering agent prompts. Select TWO controls that BEST align with this goal. (Select TWO)

options.text: Classify sensitive data sources in Unity Catalog and restrict retrieval tool access by classification tag
options.explanation: Classifying data sources in Unity Catalog and enforcing tag-based access controls prevents the retrieval tool from fetching sensitive documents in the first place.

options.text: Apply column masking policies so PII fields are redacted before being returned to the agent
options.explanation: Column masking policies redact PII at the data layer before results reach the agent, ensuring sensitive values never enter the context window.

options.text: Increase top-k retrieval to maximize coverage and reduce the chance of missing relevant records
options.explanation: Increasing top-k widens retrieval and is more likely to surface sensitive records, not less.

options.text: Require all agent responses to include a data privacy disclaimer footer
options.explanation: Response disclaimers do not prevent PII from entering the prompt; they only affect what is shown to the user after the fact.

questions.stem: A team evaluates their RAG agent only on final answer quality (correctness) and ignores retrieval diagnostics. What key operational risk does this create?

options.text: The team will automatically reduce storage costs by skipping retrieval logs
options.explanation: Evaluation scope has no direct effect on storage costs; retrieval logs are written regardless of whether they are analyzed.

options.text: The team cannot distinguish whether answer failures are caused by retrieval gaps or model generation errors, making targeted fixes impossible
options.explanation: Without retrieval diagnostics (precision, recall, chunk relevance scores), a team cannot tell whether low answer quality stems from retrieval returning the wrong chunks or from the model generating poor text from good chunks. Without this distinction, fixes are guesswork.

options.text: The team will achieve better tool routing performance as a side effect
options.explanation: Tool routing is unrelated to answer-only evaluation; measuring it requires separate tool-selection diagnostics.

options.text: The team can eliminate the need for any offline evaluation entirely
options.explanation: Ignoring retrieval diagnostics reduces evaluation coverage rather than replacing offline evaluation.

questions.stem: Unity Catalog is used to register agent tools and track which version of a tool an agent used to produce a specific answer. What governance capability does this primarily enable?

options.text: Lineage tracking: the ability to trace an agent's answer back to the exact tool version and data assets that contributed to it
options.explanation: Unity Catalog provides data and asset lineage, allowing teams to trace which tool version, data table, or model was used to produce a given output — essential for audits, debugging, and compliance.

options.text: Automatic answer correction when a newer tool version is registered in the catalog
options.explanation: Updating a tool version in the catalog does not retroactively fix or recompute past answers.

options.text: Reduction in model temperature requirements for registered tools
options.explanation: Unity Catalog is a governance layer and has no effect on model generation parameters like temperature.

options.text: Elimination of the need for vector search indexes when tools are cataloged
options.explanation: Unity Catalog and vector search serve complementary roles; registering tools in the catalog does not remove the need for retrieval indexes.

questions.stem: An agent is deployed to production and the team wants to detect prompt injection attacks where adversarial text in retrieved documents attempts to override system instructions. Which defense is MOST effective?

options.text: Clearly delimit retrieved content from instructions in the prompt and add input validation that flags or strips instruction-override patterns in retrieved text
options.explanation: Structural prompt delimiting (e.g., XML tags or explicit section markers) helps the model distinguish instructions from data, and input validation to detect and strip injection patterns in retrieved text provides a pre-insertion defense layer.

options.text: Increase the model temperature to randomize behavior and reduce attack predictability
options.explanation: Higher temperature increases output randomness, which does not prevent the model from following injected instructions and may introduce other quality issues.

options.text: Store all documents in a SQL table and never embed them in the prompt to avoid retrieval entirely
options.explanation: Storing documents in SQL without embedding them defeats the purpose of RAG; context injection is required for the model to use the documents.

options.text: Require users to manually approve every retrieved chunk before it is inserted into context
options.explanation: Requiring user approval of every chunk creates extreme friction and is not practical at production scale.

questions.stem: A team uses Databricks Agent Evaluation to assess their RAG agent. The evaluation framework reports low 'groundedness' scores. What does this indicate?

options.text: The agent's answers frequently contain claims not supported by the retrieved context, indicating hallucination
options.explanation: Groundedness measures whether the agent's answer is supported by the retrieved context. A low groundedness score means the model is making claims that cannot be traced back to the retrieved documents — a hallucination signal.

options.text: The retrieval step is returning too few results for each query
options.explanation: Retrieval volume (top-k) is a separate metric; groundedness measures answer-to-context fidelity, not retrieval breadth.

options.text: The model's generation temperature is set too low
options.explanation: Temperature affects output randomness, not whether answers are grounded in retrieved context.

options.text: The system prompt is missing a required greeting instruction
options.explanation: A missing greeting instruction is a formatting issue and would not produce low groundedness scores.

questions.stem: A team logs every agent request and response to a Delta table for monitoring. After a week, they notice the agent returns different answers to the same question on different days. Which root cause should they investigate FIRST?

options.text: Changes in the retrieval index content or model version between days, which the logged request metadata should reveal
options.explanation: Non-deterministic answers over time are most commonly explained by changes in retrieved context (index updates) or model version changes. The logged metadata for each request should include the retrieval results and model version, enabling root cause isolation.

options.text: The Delta table is compressing older rows and silently altering answer content
options.explanation: Delta table compaction affects physical storage layout, not the logical content of stored rows.

options.text: The SQL warehouse cluster is throttling requests on high-traffic days
options.explanation: Warehouse throttling affects latency, not the content of model-generated answers.

options.text: The agent's system prompt is being modified directly by end users between sessions
options.explanation: System prompt access is controlled by the application; end users cannot normally modify it directly.

questions.stem: A compliance team requires that all agent answers about regulatory topics can be traced to an approved source document. Select TWO implementation choices that BEST satisfy this requirement. (Select TWO)

options.text: Include source document identifiers in the retrieved chunks and require the agent to cite them in its response
options.explanation: Including source identifiers in retrieved chunks and instructing the agent to cite them links each answer claim to a specific approved document, satisfying the traceability requirement at the response level.

options.text: Log the retrieved chunks and their source metadata alongside each agent response in a Delta table
options.explanation: Logging retrieved chunks and their metadata (document ID, version, retrieval timestamp) alongside each response creates an auditable record that compliance reviewers can inspect.

options.text: Increase the model temperature to produce more varied and creative citations
options.explanation: Higher temperature introduces more randomness into citation generation, which would undermine traceability and could produce hallucinated citations.

options.text: Allow the model to generate citations from its pretrained knowledge without retrieval when no document matches
options.explanation: Pretrained model knowledge cannot be traced to specific approved documents; retrieval-backed citations are required for compliance.

questions.stem: A team wants to continuously monitor a deployed RAG agent for answer quality degradation as the retrieval index is updated weekly. Which approach BEST enables this?

options.text: Run a fixed evaluation benchmark against the agent after each index update and track quality metrics over time in MLflow
options.explanation: Running a consistent evaluation benchmark after each index change and tracking metrics in MLflow creates a time-series view of quality, enabling automated alerts when metrics degrade beyond a defined threshold.

options.text: Manually review 10 randomly selected answers each week and make a subjective judgment call
options.explanation: 10 random answers provide insufficient statistical power to detect subtle degradation, and the process is too manual to be operationally sustainable at scale.

options.text: Block all index updates until a dedicated QA team individually approves each new document
options.explanation: Blocking all index updates creates operational bottlenecks and does not scale with frequent content changes.

options.text: Monitor only API error rates as a proxy for overall answer quality
options.explanation: API error rates measure service availability, not answer quality; an agent can have 100% uptime and still produce degraded answers.

questions.stem: A context engineer is asked to define an acceptance threshold for deploying a new agent version. The current production agent achieves 82% groundedness and 78% answer relevance on the evaluation set. What is the MOST principled approach to setting the deployment threshold?

options.text: Set the deployment threshold at the current production metrics and require the new version to meet or exceed them on the same evaluation set before deploying
options.explanation: Using current production performance as the baseline threshold ensures new versions do not regress on the metrics that reflect user value. The new version must match or exceed the baseline to be deployed, balancing rigor with practicality.

options.text: Set the threshold at 100% for all metrics to ensure the new version is perfect before any deployment
options.explanation: 100% thresholds are unachievable for probabilistic models and would permanently block all deployments.

options.text: Skip thresholds entirely and deploy the new version if at least one metric improves over the baseline
options.explanation: Accepting a new version when only one metric improves allows regressions on other important quality dimensions, which could harm users.

options.text: Use latency as the only deployment threshold because correctness metrics are considered too noisy to trust
options.explanation: Latency alone does not capture answer quality; groundedness and relevance are equally important deployment signals for a RAG agent.
